video_title: Cooking with OpenAI’s Research Chief: AGI, o1, Evals, and Scaling Laws — Mark Chen
gist: OpenAI Chief Research Officer Mark Chen, interviewed over a cooking session, argues scaling laws and pre-training remain firmly intact (held ~10 orders of magnitude), reasoning/RL was a hard-won non-consensus bet, the field is in an 'evals crisis' as gold-standard benchmarks saturate, and OpenAI is racing toward self-sustaining 'vibe research' where models orchestrate their own end-to-end research within a three-year roadmap.

insights.claim: Mark Chen, OpenAI's Chief Research Officer, strongly disagrees with all 'pre-training is dead' and bear takes on scaling, stating scaling laws have held for nearly 10 orders of magnitude and he sees no reason they should stop holding.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: The research chief of the frontier lab is publicly betting against the scaling-plateau narrative, signaling continued capex and capability gains rather than a slowdown.
insights.quote: I firmly believe in exponent being on the exponential and in scaling laws. So, I think any of these bear takes, I fairly strongly disagree with.

insights.claim: Chen frames every prior scaling bottleneck as historically broken by better engineering, data engineering, or a research insight, arguing the 'pre-training is dead' narrative is just a recurring pattern that has always been overcome.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: Implies the next scaling unlock is an engineering/data problem to be solved, not a wall — bullish for sustained model progress.
insights.quote: we've always found some kind of technique, whether it be better engineering or some new research insight that helps you break past the boundary. And so, I think it's just more and more of the same

insights.claim: Chen lists pre-training as currently underrated, directly pushing back on consensus that the pre-training paradigm has run its course.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: Suggests OpenAI continues to invest heavily in the pre-training org even amid the RL/reasoning hype cycle.
insights.quote: if you still have a pre-training is dead view of the world, um I think uh pre-training is definitely uh yeah, yeah, yeah, not not dead. It's it's um it's underrated.

insights.claim: Chen cites reasoning (which produced o1) as the biggest example of a non-consensus bet OpenAI pushed through, noting it was hard to launch because the pre-training-plus-post-training paradigm felt so promising that people asked why disrupt a working machine.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: Shows OpenAI's edge comes from steering the org onto disruptive bets before they are obviously correct — a signal of continued willingness to cannibalize current paradigms.
insights.quote: why do something when you have a machine that works?

insights.claim: Chen credits individuals including Jakub and Ilya for having the conviction to push reasoning in earnest, noting it took a lot of steering to get the whole company behind it as a fundamental bet.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: Identifies the key personnel and internal political capital required to move a frontier lab onto a new paradigm — relevant for tracking where research leadership concentrates.

insights.claim: Chen says RL works best in domains with cold hard truth like math and computer science where answers can be proven correct or wrong, while subjective domains like creative writing remain headwinds because two experts disagree.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: Maps where AI automation will arrive first (verifiable, objective work) versus where it lags — useful for sizing disruption risk across professions.
insights.quote: for now, it's just where there's cold hard truth, things like math and computer science, where you can prove it correctly or wrong. Um that's where you kind of see it really taking off.

insights.claim: Chen positions coding/co-working as the key real-world domain testing whether models can learn in high-context, long-horizon settings, beyond saturated programming-contest benchmarks.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: Signals OpenAI views coding agents not just as a product but as the proving ground for general long-horizon capability — bullish for continued coding-agent investment.

insights.claim: Chen observes that 'move 37' breakthrough moments are now appearing across nearly every field including math, computer science and coding, with many people realizing at the start of this year that agents work in their profession.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: Frames a broad inflection in agent capability across knowledge work, supporting a rapid-automation thesis for labor-exposed sectors.
insights.quote: these models can just do long horizon meaningful work for them

insights.claim: Chen says the field is in an 'evals crisis' because the number of canonical gold-standard benchmarks is low and classics like the SAT are fully saturated, so the industry urgently needs new ways to benchmark models.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: Benchmark saturation means published scores increasingly understate frontier capability and overstate competitive parity — a reason to discount leaderboard comparisons.
insights.quote: we really are kind of in an evals crisis, right? Where all the really great uh evals that we we all know like growing up like taking the SAT or those those are all fully saturated.

insights.claim: Chen confirms 'bench maxing' is real, where a model overfits to a benchmark's distribution by overtraining on very similar instances, producing high scores that don't reflect generalization.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: Investors and buyers should treat headline benchmark wins skeptically; vibe checks and deployed behavior can diverge sharply from scores.
insights.quote: just like bench maxing, you know? Um yeah, I think I think you can kind of overfit onto certain distributions. Um and it won't be reflective how you how well you generalize

insights.claim: Chen describes OpenAI separating the teams that build evals from the teams that optimize models, creating a deliberately adversarial process so the eval team tries to build benchmarks that are hard for the models.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: Org-design detail that mitigates self-deception in capability measurement — a reusable governance model for any AI buyer trying to avoid gamed metrics.
insights.quote: separate the teams that are creating the evals from the teams that are optimizing... that way you don't like coincentivize them

insights.claim: Chen holds the philosophy that once an eval is released into the world it is already no longer a good eval, so OpenAI must continuously create new ones and operate over representative mixtures rather than single benchmarks.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: Implies a persistent moat in proprietary, freshly-built evaluation infrastructure — a hidden source of frontier-lab advantage.
insights.quote: once an eval is out in the world, then it's it's just already not a good eval

insights.claim: Chen says OpenAI partners with external organizations to craft gold-standard evals in hard math and science domains, separating eval creation from model building.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: Points to a growing market for third-party evaluation vendors serving frontier labs.

insights.claim: Chen notes tools like Codex now let a single person quickly assemble a high-quality eval, and that deploying models lets OpenAI observe real-world evals as users push the models to find where they fall over.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: Deployment scale itself becomes an evaluation moat — larger user bases yield better real-world capability signal, favoring incumbents.

insights.claim: Chen says self-sustaining research, where models do end-to-end research themselves, is one of OpenAI's big goals and the explicit end state of its three-year roadmap, with the window for human-led 'big bets' before that point being small.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: A frontier lab targeting recursive self-improvement within ~3 years reframes the AGI timeline and the value of human research talent as a depreciating input.
insights.quote: if they can kind of do self-sustaining research, this is one of the big or goals that we've set for for our research work

insights.claim: Chen believes there are still new research bets to make but probably not that many, and the key question is whether significant bets remain before models can do self-sustaining research.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: Implies the field is approaching a paradigm where compute and orchestration matter more than novel human breakthroughs — a shift in where value accrues.
insights.quote: I feel like there are new bets, but probably not that many.

insights.claim: Chen declines to frame AGI as needing two or three more breakthroughs, treating continual learning as a basic primitive to unlock rather than a paradigm shift, and says there are clearly many shots on goal that he is pretty sure will work.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: Pushes back on the 'we need several breakthroughs' camp, supporting a near-term, incremental-progress AGI thesis rather than a stalled one.
insights.quote: continual learning is a a basic primitive that you have to unlock

insights.claim: Chen identifies in-context learning — taking a single task, learning lessons, and applying them to future tasks — as a key capability humans have naturally that many AI researchers are now working toward.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: Flags the next capability frontier; labs and startups solving cross-task learning could unlock the next wave of agent value.

insights.claim: Chen invokes the 'jagged frontier' analogy, noting models can ace IMO/IOI-level problems yet struggle with mundane human tasks, partly because of missing context and because vision is more biologically wired for humans.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: Explains why superhuman benchmark scores don't translate to uniform task reliability — a caution against over-extrapolating from eval wins to deployment value.
insights.quote: there's a lot made of this jagged frontier analogy

insights.claim: Chen says both OpenAI and other labs are quickly moving toward orchestration-focused research where researchers supply ideas and the model handles implementation and execution, calling this the future of doing research.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: Research-labor productivity is being amplified by models doing execution — compresses headcount needs and accelerates lab output velocity.
insights.quote: you're starting to see a lot of the work become mostly orchestration-focused, right? Like the researchers coming up with the ideas and the model's great enough to do the implementation execution by itself.

insights.claim: Chen states models don't yet have research taste and it will be hard to teach, so human researchers are still needed for idea generation, but he predicts models will eventually reach parity on research taste.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: Research taste is the last human moat in AI R&D; its eventual automation is the trigger for recursive self-improvement and a key timeline variable.
insights.quote: It's going to be hard to teach the models good taste. We noticed that, but in terms of actually accelerating the research, there's clear tangible benefits already.

insights.claim: Chen says OpenAI's high-level research roadmap has stayed stable while implementation details, sequencing, and relative resourcing change, and the company re-examines bets at compute-allocation checkpoints.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: OpenAI is not reactive to competitor model launches; strategy is anchored and compute-gated — reduces the signal value of OpenAI 'responding' to rivals.
insights.quote: the high-level research roadmap should be stable, right? I think people need something to ground in.

insights.claim: Chen describes OpenAI's research org as three top-level domains — pre-training (world knowledge), RL (reasoning over that knowledge), and alignment/post-training — each scaling a mainline plus new bets.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: A clear map of how OpenAI organizes and resources research — useful for interpreting where the company is investing and hiring.
insights.quote: we have an org that focuses on pre-training... We focus on RL like teaching the models how to reason... And then finally um alignment and post-training

insights.claim: Chen says OpenAI is focusing its bets, tying roughly three to five bets from each org into the main roadmap, and doing more directive compute allocation by giving big swaths of compute to big bets while leaving managers flexible pools.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: A narrowing, more concentrated bet structure signals OpenAI is consolidating compute behind fewer high-conviction efforts rather than spreading thin.
insights.quote: tying would say like a small number of bets, say three to five bets from each org into the main research roadmap

insights.claim: Chen says taking a lot of high-risk bets is a big part of OpenAI's alpha and what has kept it at the frontier consistently, accepting that many bets won't pan out.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: Frames OpenAI's competitive edge as risk appetite and bet-portfolio construction rather than any single technique — relevant for assessing durability of its lead.
insights.quote: that is a big part of OpenAI's alpha because I think one thing that differentiates us from other labs is we take a lot of high-risk bets.

insights.claim: Chen favors keeping images, audio, video, and text under one unified model and as few architectures as possible, citing the underestimated cost of maintaining and scaling multiple infrastructure stacks.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: Signals OpenAI's architectural bias toward unified multimodal models over specialized ones — bearish for standalone specialized-model approaches.
insights.quote: there's a strong bias for us to keep it in as as few different archi architectures as possible.

insights.claim: Chen says OpenAI deliberately trains people without formal ML backgrounds into researchers, valuing creative out-of-the-box problem-solving over a PhD, and views the value of a research background like trading as overrated relative to general problem-solving ability.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: Widens the talent pool OpenAI competes for beyond credentialed ML PhDs — relevant for labor-market and talent-war dynamics among labs.
insights.quote: the real hard thing is the ability to creatively solve problems and think outside of the box. It's not so much, you know, you have to do a PhD

insights.claim: Chen advises aspiring researchers to develop taste through full replication of admired papers, matching exact training curves and loss values to absorb undocumented techniques.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: A concrete skill-building path; signals that hands-on replication still produces frontier-grade researchers despite model assistance.
insights.quote: the best mechanism I've found for developing that is really just replication

insights.claim: Chen says failed research bets at OpenAI still produce valuable write-ups, and identifying when a promising idea is actually less important than thought (without deluding yourself) is a critical call researchers must make.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: Knowledge capture from failures compounds a lab's research efficiency — an underappreciated source of incumbent advantage.
insights.quote: even sometimes when people fail at um proving out a technique, uh their write-ups are very important

insights.claim: Chen describes a trading-like expected-value lens on researchers: ambitious-but-sound bets that fail repeatedly are acceptable because a single mega-hit justifies the streak, but researchers must still add value on expectation.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: Reveals how OpenAI manages risk-tolerant talent — an asymmetric-payoff model that explains both its breakthroughs and its tolerance for failure.
insights.quote: they only have to justify it once in a while for it to make sense, right? It may be like a very trading like kind of lens on the world

insights.claim: Chen distinguishes implementing long context from implementing it well, noting that naively expanding the context window leaves needle-in-a-haystack failures, while compaction features in coding products compress insights or working state to shortcut expensive native long-context primitives.
insights.speaker: Mark Chen (guest, OpenAI Chief Research Officer)
insights.so_what: Compaction and state-compression, not just bigger context windows, are where long-horizon agent value is being built — a guide to which engineering approaches will win.
insights.quote: there's a difference between implementing long context and implementing long context well
